era_context: 926 CE falls within the early 10th century, a period of political fragmentation in the Islamic world, consolidation in Europe and East Asia, and the flourishing of regional powers. The Abbasid Caliphate's authority is increasingly nominal; regional dynasties are the real powers. In Europe, feudalism is solidifying, and Anglo-Saxon England is consolidating under strong kings. In East Asia, the Later Tang Dynasty is ruling northern China during the Five Dynasties period.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and North America (no written records)
Eastern Europe and the Steppe (limited sources)

events.title: Death of Edward the Elder and Accession of Athelstan
events.description: Edward the Elder, King of England (r. 899–924 CE), died in 924 CE, not 926 CE. However, 926 CE marks a critical consolidation of Athelstan's power following his accession in 927 CE. This entry is corrected: Edward the Elder died in 924 CE, and Athelstan's accession occurred in 927 CE. The year 926 CE itself has no major documented event in English history, though it falls within the transitional period between Edward's death and Athelstan's formal accession.
key_figures: Athelstan (future King of England, r. 927–939 CE)
events.certainty_note: The dating of Edward the Elder's death (924 CE) and Athelstan's accession (927 CE) is well-established. The year 926 CE has no documented major event; it is assigned by convention as a marker within the transitional period.

events.title: Reign of Li Siyuan of the Later Tang Dynasty
events.description: In 926 CE, Li Siyuan (r. 926–933 CE) became Emperor of the Later Tang Dynasty following the death of his predecessor. Li Siyuan's reign marked a period of relative stability in northern China during the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period. The year 926 CE itself marks his accession, which is well-documented in Chinese sources. Li Siyuan's reign was characterized by efforts to consolidate control over the Later Tang territories and to resist fragmentation, though the empire remained unstable and would collapse within a few years of his death.
key_figures: Li Siyuan (Later Tang Emperor, r. 926–933 CE)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled by Sima Guang in the 11th century
Official histories of the Later Tang Dynasty

events.certainty_note: Li Siyuan's accession in 926 CE is well-attested in Chinese historiography. The date is secure, though some sources may provide slightly different dates due to calendar conversion issues.
cross_references: Part of the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period (907–960 CE)

events.title: Continued Reign of Abd al-Rahman III of Al-Andalus
events.description: In 926 CE, Abd al-Rahman III (r. 912–961 CE) continued his consolidation of power over Al-Andalus. By 926 CE, he had secured control over most of the Iberian Peninsula's Islamic territories and was beginning to assert his authority as a rival caliph. The year 926 CE falls within a period of military campaigns and administrative consolidation, though no specific major event is documented for this year. Abd al-Rahman III's reign was one of the most successful of the Islamic Spanish period, marked by cultural flourishing and military strength.
key_figures: Abd al-Rahman III (Emir of Córdoba, r. 912–961 CE)
events.certainty_note: Abd al-Rahman III's reign and general chronology are well-established. The year 926 CE falls securely within his reign, but no specific event is documented for this year.
cross_references: Contemporary with 926-002 (Li Siyuan's reign in China)
Part of the broader consolidation of regional powers in the early 10th century

disconfirming_evidence: The entry on Edward the Elder's death is incorrect; he died in 924 CE, not 926 CE. Athelstan's accession occurred in 927 CE, not 926 CE. The year 926 CE itself has no major documented events in English history. This highlights the difficulty of assigning significance to years with sparse documentation.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 926 CE is sparse. Chinese sources provide good documentation for Li Siyuan's accession. Islamic sources provide moderate documentation for Abd al-Rahman III's reign. English sources are sparser for this specific year. No major events are documented for 926 CE in most regions; the year serves primarily as a chronological marker. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military history; economic, social, and cultural history are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Li Siyuan's reign ended with his death in 933 CE, leading to succession disputes
Abd al-Rahman III and the emerging Fatimid Caliphate were rival centers of Islamic authority
